title: Guidance Clarity Act of 2025
stage_label: Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs. Reported by Senator Paul with an amendment in the nature of a s
plain_summary: The Guidance Clarity Act of 2025 requires all federal agencies to put a clear warning on their guidance documents. The warning must be placed on the first page of every guidance document. It must tell people that the guidance is not a law and cannot force anyone to do anything. The warning also must explain that the guidance only helps people understand existing laws and agency rules. This helps make sure people know the difference between real laws and guidance that just explains those laws.
last_action_text: Placed on Senate Legislative Calendar under General Orders. Calendar No. 250.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Guidance Clarity Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Guidance Clarity Statement Required
sections.text: Starting thirty days after the Office of Management and Budget creates instructions for how to follow this law, all federal agencies must add a clear warning statement to their guidance documents. Federal agencies are government departments and offices that make rules and provide services to the public. Guidance documents are papers that explain how agencies interpret laws and rules, but they are not laws themselves. The warning statement must be placed prominently on the first page of every guidance document. The statement must say exactly this: The contents of this document do not have the force and effect of law and do not, of themselves, bind the public or the agency. This document is intended only to provide clarity to the public regarding existing requirements under the law or agency policies. Within ninety days of this law being passed, the Director of the Office of Management and Budget must create instructions for how agencies should follow this new requirement. The Office of Management and Budget is a government office that helps the President manage how federal agencies work.
